description: Three-layer knowledge domain schema for Eastern-to-Western philosophical bridge

approaches: Karma and rebirth as moral causation
hinduism.distinguishing_features: Non-dualism (Advaita) and diverse schools; cosmic consciousness as foundational

approaches: No permanent self; emptiness of intrinsic nature
Middle way between extremes; Four Noble Truths
buddhism.distinguishing_features: No creator god; emphasis on suffering and its cessation; enlightenment accessible to all

taoism.distinguishing_features: Emphasis on naturalness and spontaneity; critique of Confucian artificiality

confucianism.distinguishing_features: Central to East Asian political philosophy; emphasis on social harmony through cultivation

approaches: Karma as material force that binds souls
jainism.distinguishing_features: Strongest non-violence principle; no creator god; 24 Tirthankaras (enlightened beings)

approaches: Reverence for natural and ancestral spirits (kami)
Purity through ritual and nature contact
shinto.distinguishing_features: Indigenous Japanese spirituality; no fixed doctrines; kami worship central

self_identity.notes: Buddhist anatta is metaphysical negation of permanent self; Hindu Atman is cosmic consciousness; Jain jiva is eternal but bound by karma

reality_nature.notes: Emptiness as interdependence; non-dualism as Brahman; Tao as ultimate reality beyond language

consciousness.notes: Vedanta emphasizes consciousness as fundamental; Yogacara explores nature of perception

governance.notes: Confucian governance through cultivated virtue; Daoist skepticism of artificial structures; Buddhist collective wisdom

non_violence.notes: Jain ahimsa is absolute; Buddhist ahimsa includes protective action; Hindu dharma allows warrior ethics

virtue_cultivation.notes: Confucian cultivation through practice; Buddhist ethical perfections; Hindu qualities of nature

rational_knowing.notes: Confucian emphasis on study; Buddhist and Hindu philosophical logic systems

experiential_knowing.notes: Buddhist prajña transcends intellectual knowing; Hindu direct experience of Brahman; Taoist spontaneous knowing

paradoxical_knowing.notes: Zen koans exemplify paradoxical knowing; Taoist non-action; Buddhist sudden enlightenment

governance_systems.notes: Confucian state governance through virtue; Daoist critique of artificial structures; Buddhist collective decision-making

statecraft.notes: Confucian ethical governance; Hindu pragmatic statecraft; Daoist non-action in politics

spiritual_art.notes: Zen minimalism and impermanence; Hindu rasa theory of aesthetic emotion; Taoist natural aesthetics

beauty_nature.notes: Taoist alignment with natural forms; Shinto sacred landscape; Buddhist appreciation of impermanence

layer_3_jungian_frameworks.description: Jungian psychology as bridge to Western comprehension (NOT reduction of Eastern meaning)

archetypal_correspondences.wise_old_man: Taoist sage (wu-wei master); Hindu guru (knowledge transmitter); Confucian junzi (cultivated elder)
archetypal_correspondences.shadow: Hindu/Buddhist shadow recognition (avidya/ignorance); Jungian integration as parallel Western process
archetypal_correspondences.anima_animus: Yin-yang balance; Hindu Shiva-Shakti (masculine-feminine cosmic principles)
archetypal_correspondences.self: Hindu Atman; Buddhist Buddha-nature; Confucian junzi as actualized potential

psychological_processes.individuation: Confucian self-cultivation (becoming junzi); Hindu yoga paths (actualization of potential); Buddhist path to enlightenment
psychological_processes.integration: Buddhist integration of opposing forces; Hindu balanced gunas; Taoist yin-yang harmony
psychological_processes.unconscious_contents: Buddhist working with mental formations (samskaras); Hindu prarabdha karma (enacted karma); Taoist shadow wu-wei

layer_3_jungian_frameworks.collective_unconscious: Hindu/Buddhist collective karma (samsara); Shinto kami (collective spirits); Confucian ancestral wisdom tradition
layer_3_jungian_frameworks.critical_note: Jung provides comprehension bridges for Western psychology, NOT philosophical reduction. Buddhist anatta is metaphysical ontology, not merely psychological integration. Use Jung for Western clarity only.

curator_guidance.when_to_use_single_tradition: When topic-tradition affinity shows clear primary (e.g., governance → Confucianism). Present primary with depth; acknowledge secondary traditions.
curator_guidance.when_to_present_all_equally: Rare: Only for comparative/overview questions (e.g., 'How do Eastern traditions view the self?'). Even then, emphasize authentic differences.
curator_guidance.when_to_use_jungian_bridges: Only when Western audience needs comprehension aid. Flag explicitly: 'For Western psychological comprehension...' Never present as equivalent.
curator_guidance.when_to_highlight_contradictions: Always when traditions contradict (anatta vs Atman, Confucian vs Daoist governance). Present both authentically; don't harmonize.
curator_guidance.validation_check: Would a scholar of this Eastern tradition recognize and approve my representation? If unsure, lean toward traditional sources (Key Texts), not synthesis.
